47357979.title: Introduction to Java Streams
47357979.transcript: Hi everyone. Welcome back. In this lecture, let us quickly take a look into introduction to Java streams. Well, Java streams were introduced in Java eight to support the functional style programming in Java. Well, what is stream? A stream is a sequence of data elements for processing. Well, we can use stream to process the data. Like we can use stream to filter the data, map the data, sort the data and reduce the collection of data. Okay. So stream allows you to perform data processing operations such as filtering, mapping and reducing on a collection of data. Well probably you already heard about the collections, right. So collections we use to store the data but stream we use to process the data. So we we do not use stream to store the data okay. So we simply use stream to process the data like filtering, mapping, sorting, reducing etc. streams can be used with various data sources including arrays, collections, and even files. Well if you look at the diagram over here. So this diagram basically summarizes the end to end stream pipeline. So basically we create a stream object from the sources like array or collection or I o channel. Once we create a stream object from the source. Next we can perform the intermediate operations on a stream like filtering, sorting type, conversion, mapping, etc. as the intermediate operations return the another stream. As a result, they can be chained together to form a pipeline of operations. So why should we use streams? Well, there are two main reasons. First, streams makes your code much simpler and easier to read. Instead of writing long loops or if statements streams let you use few simple methods to achieve the same result. Second streams allows you to process large amounts of data efficiently. They are built to work with big datasets, which is important if you are dealing with large lists or databases. Now let us talk about how streams are different from collections like list or set. You might be familiar with collections in Java like ArrayList or Hashset etc. collections are used to store and manage the data. But streams are different. They don't store data themselves. Instead, streams are used to process the data like filtering, mapping, sorting, etc.. Streams let you work with data that's already inside the collection without modifying the original data. Next, one more key difference is collections can be reused. On the other hand. Streams are one time use. Well, when you work with collections, you can go through the data as many times as you want. But with streams, once you process the stream data, the stream is consumed. If you need to work with the data again, you will have to create a new stream. Okay, another important difference is how data is processed. Collections use eager loading, which means all data is stored in memory at once, but streams use lazy loading only processing data as needed. This helps improve performance, especially with large data sets. Next, collections are imperative. Style on the other hand. Streams supports functional style well. Streams supports functional style operations. This means you can easily apply transformations, filters and aggregation to the data instead of writing loops or if statements. You can change the methods like map or filter to achieve the same result with fewer lines of code. Lastly, collections are designed for data management, allowing you to add, remove, or update the elements directly. On the other hand, streams don't allow any direct modification. They are specifically designed for data processing. Your original collection stays unchanged, which helps keep data handling simpler and error free. All right, so these are the key differences between streams and collections. Collections are perfect for storing and managing data, while streams are designed for efficient and functional style data processing without modifying the original collection. All right. So we'll understand, you know, these differences in depth going forward. Okay. Great. Now let us talk about some key features of streams that make them powerful. First, streams are not data structures as I mentioned earlier. They don't hold data. They just allow you to work with data that's in the collections. Second, streams are lazy. This means streams don't do any work until you tell them to. You can think of it like setting up a receive, but actual cooking does not start until you give the final command. Third streams are read only. This means streams don't change the data they are processing. Instead, they create a new results based on the data without modifying the original collections. So these are the key features of Java streams. So this is a quick introduction to Java streams. In the next lecture we will take a look into types of stream operations. All right great I will see you in the next lecture.

As a result, they can be chained together to form a pipeline of operations and again the intermediate operations are lazily loaded. And as the name suggests, intermediate operations does not give the end result. They just transform one stream to another stream. In order to get the end result, we have to call the terminal operation. Next. Once the terminal operation is called, the stream is consumed and cannot be used again. If you want to use the stream again, you have to create a new stream from the source. Okay, so this is the complete stream pipeline. Stream pipelines are a powerful way to work with the data in a Java, because they allow you to process the data in a clean, readable, and efficient way. By chaining intermediate operations, you can write a code that is easy to understand and by waiting to execute until the terminal operation, Java streams avoid unnecessary work. This is especially useful when dealing with large data sets, or when you need to perform multiple transformations on your data.

47358197.title: Understanding Stream collect() Method
47358197.transcript: Hi everyone. Welcome back. In this lecture let us discuss stream provided collect method. Well the collect method is used to gather the elements of a stream into a container such as list, set or map. Well, basically we use a collect method to collect the result of a stream into a different data structure like list, set or map. The collect is a terminal operation. This means that the stream pipeline ends once the collect method is called. Well, the collect method in Java Stream is a terminal operation that helps gather the elements of a stream into a desired data structure like list, set, or map. When you reach the collect method in a stream pipeline, it processes all the elements and creates a final result based on the specific instructions. Okay, so once we call the collect method on a stream, then the stream pipeline ends and you cannot consume that stream again. Next, to make a collecting data easier, Java provides a set of built in collectors which are helper methods that define how to collect the elements. For example, you can use collectors dot to list method to collect all stream elements into a list. You can use collectors dot to set method to collect all the stream elements into set, and you can use collectors dot two map method to collect all the stream elements as a key value pairs. Next, beyond basic collections collect method also perform more complex operations like grouping the elements by certain attributes with collectors, dot grouping by method or dividing elements into two groups with collectors dot partitioning by method. Well, most of the time we use collectors utility class with a collect method to perform all these operations like grouping, partitioning, collecting the stream into a list, set or map. So we can call the terminal operation like collect method. So once we call the collect method then this collect method basically triggers the intermediate operations. Well streams are lazy. It means the intermediate operations won't get executed immediately. Once we call the terminal operation, then only these intermediate operations will get executed. 48848571.title: ArrayList vs LinkedList
48848571.transcript: Now we pretty much understood about linked list. Next, let us take a look into the difference between array list and linked list. Well, both array list and linked list implements the list interface. However, they differ completely in the way they store and link to the elements and array list internally uses array to store the elements sequentially based on their index. However, a linked list uses doubly linked list to store its elements, so you can see the the data structure. The array list and linked list uses array list. Internal uses array to store the elements based on their index and linked list. Internally uses doubly linked list to store the elements. Okay, next, a linked list consumes more memory than array list because it also stores the next and previous references along with the data. Well, if we can see the diagram over here. So this is the array list representation and array list internal uses array to store the elements. And you can see array list uses the indexes to point to the elements and index starts with zero. And here you can see. Linked list representation. So linked list uses doubly linked list. Doubly linked list is nothing but a collection of nodes where each node contains three fields. The middle field contains the data, and this field contains the, you know, memory location of the previous node, and this field contains the memory location of the next node. As we can clearly see, that doubly linked list occupies more memory because it stores the location of previous and next nodes as well as the data, isn't it? That's why the linked list consumes more memory than array list. Next, adding or removing elements are usually slower in an array list compared to linked list. Well, this is because the elements in the array list needs to be shifted. If a new element is added in the middle of the array list, the array list might also need to be resized to accommodate the new element. Similarly, in case of removal, all the elements in ArrayList needs to be shifted to a new positions. That is why adding or removing elements are usually slower in a ArrayList compared to linked list. So linked list is quite faster in case of adding or removing the elements because there is no shifting required. All right, perfect ArrayList class can act as list only because it implements list interface. Linked list class acts as both list and queue as it implements list and dequeue interfaces. Well, as we can clearly see in the hierarchy, ArrayList only acts as a list, but linked list can act as a list as well as queue. Because linked list implements both list and dequeue interfaces and dequeue interface internally extends queue interface. Okay, so these are the important differences between ArrayList and linked list. Okay. In next lecture we will learn more about linked list class with lots of examples. All right great I will see you in the next lecture.

48876809.transcript: Hi everyone. Welcome back. In this lecture let us quickly understand what is Java Collections Framework and its hierarchy. First things first. What is Java Collections? Well, Java Collections is a framework that provides free built data structures for storing and managing data efficiently. This Java collections framework includes interfaces, implementations, and utility classes. So instead of manually managing the arrays, Java Collections provides dynamic, flexible and optimized inbuilt data structures. Now let us take a look into the Java Collections Framework hierarchy. So if you can look at this diagram over here. This diagram represents the Java Collections Framework hierarchy. And at the top you can see the iterable interface. So iterable is an interface that represents a collection of elements that can be iterated over. Meaning you can access the elements one by one. And this iterable interface provides the capability of iteration or looping for all the extended collections. And here we have a collection interface. So collection interface is a main interface I mean it is a root interface in the Java Collections Framework hierarchy. A collection basically represents a group of objects known as elements, and this collection interface contains all the common methods. Then that can be accessed by the extended interfaces. Next here we have a list interface. List interface extends a collection interface, and this list interface represents ordered collection of elements that can allow the duplicate elements and list interface has the common implementations like ArrayList, link list vector. And here we have a set. Set. Interface extends collection interface set represents unordered collection of unique elements. It means set does not allow the duplicate elements. And here you can see set interface has the common implementations like hash set, linked hash set. And here we have a sorted set interface that extends set interface. So this sorted set interface contains the elements that are in ascending order. And this set class implements this sorted set interface. Next here we have a queue Q interface that extends collection interface. This Q represents a collection that follows first in first order. This Q interface has a common implementations such as priority queue and linked list. And here we have a dequeue interface. The Duke extends the queue interface. It is a double ended queue that allows adding and removing elements from both the ends. The common implementations of dequeue interface are array, dequeue and linked list. And here we have blocking queue that extends queue interface and the common implementations of blocking queue interface are array blocking queue and linked blocking queue. Next here we have a map hierarchy. Well, map does not extend the collection interface, but it is considered as a part of the collections framework. This map represents a collection of key value pairs. This map interface has a common implementations such as hash map, linked hash map and hash table. Hash table is a legacy class. Next here we have a sorted map interface that extends map interface. In a sorted map interface, the elements. I mean, the keys are sorted in ascending order and the sorted map interface has the implementation called tree map. So this is the complete hierarchy of collections framework. Next, in this collections framework you can see the legacy classes hash table vector stack. So these are the legacy classes. And these are the rarely used in a Java development. So this is how the Java Collections framework looks at a high level. In next lecture onwards we will learn in depth about the commonly used Java collections. So we will mainly focus on ArrayList. Linked list. Hash set, linked hash set, tree set, hash map, linked hash map and tree map. So these are the commonly used Java collection implementations in a Java project development.
